elements.text3: ‘Sin is [our] refusal to admit our creatureliness. We pretend to be more than we are.’ — Reinhold Niebuhr

elements.question3: What do you think of this quotation? (Do you like it or not, and why?)

elements.text3: Sin as Refusal
The Bible has many ways of describing sin, including as types of refusal.

elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness, <sup>19</sup>because what can be known about God is plain to them, because God has made it plain to them. <sup>20</sup>For since the creation of the world his invisible attributes—his eternal power and divine nature—have been clearly seen, because they are understood through what has been made. So people are without excuse. <sup>21</sup>For although they knew God, they did not glorify him as God or give him thanks, but they became futile in their thoughts and their senseless hearts were darkened. <sup>22</sup>Although they claimed to be wise, they became fools <sup>23</sup>and exchanged the glory of the immortal God for an image resembling mortal human beings or birds or four-footed animals or reptiles.

elements.question3: What do we learn about how sin can lead to a refusal of knowledge?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says that knowledge of God is clearly available to everyone through creation — his eternal power and divine nature are plain for all to see. Yet people actively suppress this truth. Rather than receiving the knowledge God has made available, sin drives people to refuse it — their thinking becomes futile, their hearts darkened, and they end up trading the glory of the immortal God for idols. Sin doesn’t just make people ignorant; it makes them actively resistant to what they already, at some level, know.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says God has made knowledge of himself ’plain’ — so why, according to verses 21-23, do people end up in darkness despite that?

elements.passageText5: <sup>2</sup>For if the message spoken through angels had credibility, and every transgression and act of disobedience received a just reward, <sup>3</sup>how will we escape if we neglect such a great salvation? It was first communicated through the Lord and was confirmed to us by those who heard him,

elements.question3: And how else can sin lead to a refusal of knowledge?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Here the refusal of knowledge takes the form of neglect. The author warns that if even the old message given through angels carried serious consequences for those who disobeyed, how much more serious is it to ignore the great salvation announced by the Lord himself? Sin can lead us to drift past saving knowledge not through dramatic rejection, but through simple carelessness — paying it no attention.
elements.questionHint3: The writer doesn’t say people ’rejected’ salvation but that they ’neglected’ it — what is the difference, and why might neglect still be a form of refusing knowledge?

elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>Little children, let no one deceive you: The one who practices righteousness is righteous, just as Jesus is righteous.

elements.question3: And how else?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John warns against being deceived about the connection between character and conduct. Sin can involve a refusal to accept what we actually know — namely, that a genuinely righteous person is known by actually practising righteousness. We can deceive ourselves into thinking we know God while our behaviour tells a different story. This is a refusal of knowledge about what true righteousness looks like.
elements.questionHint3: John says ’let no one deceive you’ — what specific piece of knowledge is someone at risk of being deceived about in this verse?

elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>Yet death reigned from Adam until Moses even over those who did not sin in the same way as Adam (who is a type of the coming one).

elements.question3: In what way(s) is God’s authority refused in this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul makes the point that death — the consequence of Adam’s refusal to obey God — reigned even over people who had no specific commandment to break in the way Adam did. Adam’s sin was a direct defiance of a known command; but even where no such explicit law existed, the pattern of sin as a refusal of God’s right to rule had already been set. The universal reign of death is evidence that humanity as a whole has been living in refusal of God’s authority since the beginning.
elements.questionHint3: If death reigned even over those who hadn’t broken a specific command like Adam, what does that tell you about how deeply Adam’s original act of refusal has affected everyone?

elements.passageText5: <sup>3</sup>He said to me, ‘Son of man, I am sending you to the house of Israel, to rebellious nations who have rebelled against me; both they and their fathers have revolted against me to this very day. <sup>4</sup>The people to whom I am sending you are obstinate and hard-hearted, and you must say to them, "This is what the Sovereign Lord says." <sup>5</sup>As for them, whether they listen or not—for they are a rebellious house—they will know that a prophet has been among them.

elements.question3: And in what way(s) is God’s authority refused in this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: God describes Israel as rebellious, obstinate, and hard-hearted — and notes that this rebellion has continued across generations (‘they and their fathers’). Their refusal is not passive indifference but active, persistent revolt against the Sovereign Lord. Even the possibility that they won’t listen is anticipated and accepted by God, who sends Ezekiel anyway. Sin here is a deliberate, multigenerational refusal to submit to God’s word and authority.
elements.questionHint3: God uses several strong words to describe Israel’s attitude — ‘rebellious’, ‘obstinate’, ‘hard-hearted’ — what pattern do those words together describe about how they have treated God’s authority?

elements.passageText5: For although they knew God, they did not glorify him as God or give him thanks, 21but they became futile in their thoughts and their senseless hearts were darkened.

elements.question3: Sin can also involve being ungrateful. What is refused in this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: What is refused here is twofold: the glorifying of God as God, and thankfulness to him. Even people who knew God chose not to honour him as God or to give thanks. Ingratitude is not merely a social failing — Paul frames it as a form of sin, a refusal to acknowledge God’s goodness and give him the credit he is due. The consequences are serious: futile thinking and a darkened heart.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says they ’knew God’ but still refused two specific things — can you name both of them, and what do they have in common?

elements.passageText5: <sup>40</sup>But if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their ancestors—their unfaithfulness by which they were unfaithful to me and also how they walked in opposition to me—

elements.question3: How did the Israelites refuse to be in relationship with God?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The verse speaks of their unfaithfulness — they were unfaithful to God and walked in opposition to him. Relationship with God requires faithfulness and loyalty, but they refused both. Walking ’in opposition’ to God is a vivid image of actively moving against him rather than with him. The fact that this pattern needed to be confessed generation by generation shows how deeply embedded the refusal of relationship had become.
elements.questionHint3: The passage uses two phrases to describe what Israel did — ‘unfaithful’ and ’walked in opposition’ — what kind of relationship does each phrase picture, and what does refusing both look like?

elements.passageText5: <sup>25</sup>They exchanged the truth of God for a lie and worshiped and served the creation rather than the Creator, who is blessed forever! Amen.

elements.question3: Ultimately, sin is a refusal of God himself. How do we see that?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes the ultimate form of sin as an exchange: swapping the truth of God for a lie, and transferring worship and service away from the Creator to created things. This is not just breaking rules — it is the wholesale replacement of God himself with something else. The creature refuses the Creator: they don’t merely disobey God, they redirect the devotion that is his due to things he has made. That is the deepest form of refusal.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses the word ’exchanged’ — what exactly was exchanged for what, and what does that tell you about where their ultimate loyalty ended up?

elements.passageText5: <sup>30</sup>Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your mind, and with all your strength.’

elements.question3: What is the opposite of refusing God?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The opposite of refusing God is loving him with everything we have — heart, soul, mind, and strength. Where sin withholds, the greatest commandment demands total, wholehearted devotion. The fourfold repetition of ’all’ makes clear that this is not partial loyalty or occasional attention, but a complete and unreserved orientation of our entire selves towards God.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus says ’all’ four times in this verse — what does that repeated emphasis tell you about what total acceptance of God (the opposite of refusal) actually looks like?

elements.text3: We refuse God, or we love Him with everything we’ve got. Sin is refusing the good that God wants for us, and trying to grasp our own ’good’ instead. There are many opportunities for refusal: we can refuse knowledge of God, His authority, His goodness. We can even refuse God Himself.

elements.question3: Which of these types of refusals are you most at risk of falling into?

elements.text3: The Effects of Sin

elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness,

elements.question3: What effects does sin have on our relationship with God?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sin draws the wrath of God. Paul says the wrath of God is currently being revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness — specifically against those who suppress the truth. Sin does not leave the relationship with God neutral; it places us under his just anger. The suppression of truth is not an innocent act — it is the active distortion of what God has made plain, and it has real consequences for how God relates to us.
elements.questionHint3: What specific response does Paul say God has towards ungodliness and unrighteousness — and does that suggest the relationship between God and the sinner is comfortable or troubled?

elements.passageText5: <sup>31</sup>because he has set a day on which he is going to judge the world in righteousness, by a man whom he designated, having provided proof to everyone by raising him from the dead.’

elements.question3: And what else does sin do to our relationship with God?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sin brings us into the category of those who will face God’s judgement. Paul declares that God has already fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness, through the risen Jesus. Every person living in sin stands under the certainty of that coming judgement. The resurrection of Jesus is the proof that this is real — it is not a distant threat but a confirmed appointment.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says God has ’set a day’ — what does the fact that this day is already fixed tell you about the certainty of judgement for those who continue in sin?

elements.passageText5: <sup>21</sup>And you were at one time strangers and enemies in your minds as expressed through your evil deeds,

elements.question3: And what else does sin do to our relationship with God?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sin makes us strangers and enemies of God. Paul says this estrangement is not just external — it originates in the mind, and it expresses itself through evil deeds. Sin produces a fundamental alienation from God: we become not just distant from him but hostile to him. The two words Paul uses — ‘strangers’ and ’enemies’ — describe both the relational distance and the active opposition that sin creates.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses two words to describe our pre-reconciliation relationship with God — ‘strangers’ and ’enemies’ — what is the difference between them, and which is more serious?

elements.passageText5: For we too were once foolish, disobedient, misled, enslaved to various passions and desires, spending our lives in evil and envy, hateful and hating one another—3but when the kindness of God our Savior and his love for mankind appeared,

elements.question3: How does sin affect our relationship with other people, and creation?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul paints a bleak picture: sin leads to lives characterised by evil and envy, and a state of being hateful and hating one another. Relationships with others are poisoned — instead of love and goodwill, there is mutual hostility. Sin does not just damage our relationship with God; it corrupts every human relationship, turning people who were made for community into sources of harm to one another.
elements.questionHint3: Paul ends his description with ’hateful and hating one another’ — what does that double description suggest about how sin affects both what we are and what we do to those around us?

elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>Do not trust a friend; do not rely on a companion. Guard your words from the one who lies in your embrace.

elements.question3: And how else does sin affect our relationship with other people?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sin destroys trust — even between the closest of people. Micah describes a world so corrupted by sin that you cannot trust your closest friend, your companion, or even the person in your own home. This is a picture of social breakdown at the most intimate level: sin reaches into the places where trust should be strongest and corrodes them. Relationships that should be safe become dangerous.
elements.questionHint3: Micah moves from ’friend’ to ’companion’ to ’the one who lies in your embrace’ — what does this progression tell you about how far sin’s damage to trust reaches?

elements.passageText5: <sup>13</sup>But evil people and charlatans will go from bad to worse, deceiving others and being deceived themselves.

elements.question3: And how else does sin affect our relationship with other people?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sin spreads through deception — people who are in sin deceive others and are themselves deceived. This is a self-reinforcing cycle: those who are evil and those who are charlatans do not stay the same, they go from bad to worse. Sin damages relationships with others by turning them into occasions for manipulation and deception, while also trapping the sinner deeper in their own self-deception.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says evil people are both ’deceiving others’ and ’being deceived themselves’ — what does it mean that sin damages both what you do to others and what happens to you?

elements.passageText5: <sup>3:16</sup>For where there is envy and selfish ambition, there is disorder and every evil practice. <sup>4:1</sup>Where do the conflicts and where do the quarrels among you come from? Is it not from this, that your passions are at war within your members? <sup>2</sup>You desire and you do not have; you murder and envy and you cannot obtain; you quarrel and fight. You do not have because you do not ask.

elements.question3: And how else does sin affect our relationship with other people?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: James traces conflict and quarrel directly back to internal sinful desires. Where there is envy and selfish ambition, disorder and every evil practice follow. The passions that war inside us spill outward into conflict with others — fighting, quarrelling, and even murder. Sin does not stay internal; it becomes the engine of relational breakdown and social disorder. The root is within; the fruit poisons those around us.
elements.questionHint3: James asks where quarrels ’come from’ and then answers his own question — what does his answer reveal about the real source of conflict between people?

elements.passageText5: <sup>22</sup>Do not rob the poor because he is poor, and do not crush the afflicted at the city gate,

elements.question3: And how else does sin affect our relationship with other people?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sin drives the powerful to exploit the powerless. The proverb warns against robbing the poor precisely because of their poverty, and crushing the afflicted at the very place (the city gate) where they should receive justice. Sin corrupts relationships across social lines — it turns those with power into oppressors of the vulnerable, reversing the justice and care that God intends.
elements.questionHint3: The verse says not to rob the poor ’because he is poor’ — what does that phrase suggest about the particularly sinful nature of exploiting someone’s vulnerability?

elements.passageText5: 3For we too were once foolish, disobedient, misled, enslaved to various passions and desires, spending our lives in evil and envy, hateful and hating one another.—but when the kindness of God our Savior and his love for mankind appeared,

elements.question3: What effects does sin have on our self?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes sin’s effects on the self as comprehensive: it makes us foolish (poor judgement), disobedient (rebellious will), and misled (distorted thinking). Most damagingly, it enslaves us — we become captive to various passions and desires, with no real freedom. Sin does not liberate the self; it imprisons it, reducing the person to a slave of their own cravings.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses the word ’enslaved’ to describe one of sin’s effects on the self — what does slavery imply about how much freedom or control the sinner actually has over their own life?

elements.question3: And what else does sin do to our self?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sin also deceives the self. Paul says that evil people go from bad to worse and are not just deceiving others but being deceived themselves. The sinner does not see their situation clearly — they are caught in a self-reinforcing delusion. Sin doesn’t just make us behave badly; it warps our perception so that we can no longer accurately assess our own condition, making the problem harder and harder to escape.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says that along with deceiving others, sinful people are ’being deceived themselves’ — who or what is doing the deceiving, and what might that mean for the sinner’s ability to see themselves clearly?

elements.text3:
Christians can create hierarchies of sin, which are based more on what is respectable within their culture than on the Bible’s values. External and blatant sins are bad, but ’invisible’ sins are overlooked. For example, sexual sin is ’bad,‘ but pride, greed, and jealousy are tolerated and/or excused. Jesus did not conform to respectable hierarchies of sin.

That’s why the Pharisees hated Him, but the ’sinners’ listened (Mark 2:15-17). Don’t line yourself up with the Pharisees in that argument! You won’t grow if you don’t repent of your sin. Repentance is how you master the predator crouching at your door, that desires to have you. Turn from sin, and turn to Christ — that is how to kill sin before it kills you.
Reflection Questions

elements.question3: What sins have you fallen into today, that you could repent of?
What would repentance/restitution in this area of your life look like?
Are there ongoing sins that are currently mastering your life? Which ones?
If so, you may need help to master them. Who could help you?

chapters.chapterTitle3: Grow in the Father’s Love (part i)

elements.text3: ‘God is a good Father. He doesn’t make us earn His love. He gives it freely and asks us to receive it.’ — Anon.

elements.question3: What do you think of this quotation? (Do you like it or not, and why?)

elements.text3: Your Understanding of How God Sees You

Here are 7 pairs of statements. Each pair defines opposite ends of a scale describing your view of God’s attitude towards you. On each line, choose the point that best represents your idea of how God sees you. The closer the point is to the statement, the more you think it’s like that.

elements.instruction: For each pair of statements, choose the point on the scale that best represents how you think God sees you.

statementPairs.left: He accepts me despite my faults
statementPairs.right: He rejects me because of my faults

statementPairs.left: He has forgiven me for everything
statementPairs.right: He has not forgiven some things
He ignores where my life is going

statementPairs.left: He is passionately interested in me
statementPairs.right: He is not interested in me at all

statementPairs.left: He looks at me with joy
statementPairs.right: He looks at me with disappointment

elements.text3: The Bible often describes God as our Father. Sometimes we turn to our own (earthly) fathers to understand what this means. Some will have done a better job than others. But the Bible is the right place to look to understand what sort of Father God is. It shows us where we stand before Him, and how He sees us. A big factor in that, is the question of whether we have come home to God.
The Lost Son (Luke 15:11-32)
Read through the parable of the lost son, and then answer these questions.
Character 1: The Younger Son

elements.passageText5: <sup>12</sup>The younger of them said to his father, ‘Father, give me the share of the property that will belong to me.’ So he divided the estate between them.

elements.question3: What is unusual, or even rude, about the younger son’s behaviour? And what is surprising about the father’s response?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: In the culture of the day, asking for your inheritance while your father is still alive was deeply insulting — it was effectively wishing your father were dead. The son wants the father’s wealth without the relationship. What is surprising is the father’s response: rather than rebuking the son or refusing, he divides the estate and gives it to him. The father does not coerce loyalty; he allows the son to go, even at great personal cost.
elements.questionHint3: In the culture of the time, an inheritance was only received after a parent’s death — what does the son’s demand therefore imply about how he views his father and their relationship?

elements.passageText5: <sup>13</sup>After a few days, the younger son gathered together all he had and traveled to a distant country, where he squandered his wealth with extravagant living.

elements.question3: How does the son use his freedom?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: He leaves quickly, travels as far away as possible from his father, and wastes everything he has been given on extravagant, reckless living. The freedom he demanded is used entirely for self-indulgence, with no thought for his father, his family, or the future. He converts relationship and inheritance into immediate personal pleasure.
elements.questionHint3: Notice the son goes to ’a distant country’ — what does the physical distance from his father suggest about the kind of freedom he was really looking for?

elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>When he had spent everything, a severe famine took place in that country, and he began to be in need. <sup>15</sup>So he went and worked for one of the citizens of that country, who sent him to his fields to feed pigs.

elements.question3: He gets to do everything he wants, but how does it end up?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: He runs out of money, a famine hits, and he finds himself in desperate need. Rather than being his own master, he is now entirely dependent on a stranger who sends him to feed pigs — the most degrading work imaginable for a Jewish young man. The freedom he chased has brought him not to abundance but to poverty, servitude, and humiliation.
elements.questionHint3: The son wanted to be free and in control of his own life — looking at where he ends up in verse 15, how much freedom and control does he actually have now?

elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>He was wanting to eat the pods that the pigs were eating, but no one gave him anything.

elements.question3: What is tragic about this, especially for a Jewish boy?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: He has reached the absolute lowest point. Pigs were considered unclean animals under Jewish law, and contact with them was something a Jewish person would have considered deeply defiling. Now he is not just feeding them but longing to eat what they eat — and even that is denied him. The boy who demanded his father’s wealth is now below the level of the pigs he tends. He has lost everything, including his dignity.
elements.questionHint3: Pigs were considered ritually unclean in Jewish culture — what does the fact that a Jewish boy is not only tending them but wanting to eat their food tell you about how far he has fallen?

elements.text3:
Perhaps you can identify with this young man. It’s been many people’s story. God made us, and loves us, but we take it all for granted. God gets in our way. So, in our different ways, we leave home. We turn our backs on the God who loves us. Some of us march out of God’s front door: ‘I’ll be my own boss. I’ll do what I want, when I want.’

Others leave quietly through the back door. If that’s you, then you probably still believe in God, and go to church. But when it comes to important things, you do what you want; not what God wants. You have not made a fuss about it, but you are still far away from God — doing your own thing without reference to Him. That is what sin is — doing your own thing, forgetting about God, ignoring His standards, and living in His world without paying Him any real attention.

elements.question3: Overall, how do you feel in your relationship with God? Do you mostly feel ’at home’ with Him, or do you feel more distant?

elements.text3: Sin doesn’t always make you feel dreadful, not straight away. Sin can be fun. We all know that, because we have all enjoyed it. But it is a hollow sort of fun. We need more and more to satisfy us, and it is still never enough. Long-term we end up destroying ourselves. We end up in the pig-house. It’s no surprise it turns out like that when we reject God’s standards. He made us, He knows how we should live. Sin means making myself the most important thing in the universe — and that always brings with it bitterness, envy, and hatred. We hurt ourselves and we hurt others.
Character 2: The Loving Father

elements.passageText5: 19I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me like one of your hired men.’ 20So he got up and went to his father. But while he was still a long way off his father saw him and felt compassion for him, and ran and embraced his neck and kissed him.

elements.question3: What sort of reception does the son expect to get from his father?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The son expects to be demoted. He rehearses a speech admitting he is no longer worthy to be called a son and asks only to be treated as a hired servant. He expects a business transaction at best — not restoration. He is coming home not because he expects love but because he is desperate and has nowhere else to go. His expectations of his father are low.
elements.questionHint3: What is the son planning to ask his father to make him — and what does that tell you about what kind of welcome he thinks he deserves?

elements.text3: We only get to know people on the outside. We worry that if other people knew all that we have thought, said, and done, then they would wish they didn’t! And if we are worried about what other people might think, then we certainly don’t want God to know what we are really like. But this story tells us that even though God knows everything about us, He still loves us and wants us back. There has never been a day when God has not thought of us; looking for some sign that we will come home.

elements.passageText5: ’I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me like one of your hired men.’ So he got up and went to his father. But while he was still a long way off20his father saw him and felt compassion for him, and ran and embraced his neck and kissed him.

elements.question3: Do you think the father has given up on his son? Why / Why not?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: No. The detail that the father saw him ’while he was still a long way off’ implies he was watching and waiting — he had not given up hope. He does not wait for the son to arrive and make his case; he runs to him. A father who had given up would not be scanning the horizon. His immediate compassion and physical embrace show that his love has never wavered, despite everything his son did.
elements.questionHint3: How was the father able to see his son ’while he was still a long way off’ — and what does that suggest about what the father had been doing during his son’s absence?

elements.question3: Sum up the father’s response in this verse, when he realises his son has come home.
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The father’s response is immediate, physical, and overwhelming: he feels compassion, runs to his son, embraces him, and kisses him — all before the son has said a word. There is no hesitation, no lecture, no conditions. The return of his son produces an outpouring of love that completely overtakes any thought of reproach.
elements.questionHint3: The verse describes four actions the father takes in quick succession — can you list them, and what do they together tell you about the father’s emotional state?

elements.passageText5: <sup>22</sup>But the father said to his slaves, ‘Hurry! Bring out the best robe and put it on him! Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet! <sup>23</sup>Bring the fattened calf and kill it! Let us eat and celebrate,

elements.question3: And what is the father’s response in these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The father immediately restores the son to full honour: the best robe (not a servant’s), a ring (a sign of family authority and belonging), sandals (servants went barefoot, not sons), and a feast with the fattened calf reserved for special occasions. The son asked to be a servant; the father reinstates him as a son. Everything about the response is lavish, urgent, and joyful — nothing is held back.
elements.questionHint3: The son asked to be treated like a hired servant — look at the robe, ring, and sandals the father gives him. What status does each item represent, and how does it compare to what the son requested?

elements.passageText5: <sup>24</sup>because this son of mine was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found!’ So they began to celebrate.

elements.question3: And what is the father’s response in this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The father explains his joy in the most profound terms: his son was dead and is alive again, was lost and is found. He does not describe his son’s return in terms of behavioural improvement or a debt repaid — he describes it as a resurrection, a recovery of something precious that was gone. This is not relief; it is overwhelming joy at a life restored. And so they celebrate.
elements.questionHint3: The father doesn’t say ’my son was bad but now he’s better’ — he says ’he was dead and is alive again.’ What does that stronger language tell you about how the father understood the son’s time away?

elements.text3: God wants us back, and, to show His love for us, He sent us His Son to take our place. That’s God’s great declaration to the world — He loves us and wants us home. Jesus didn’t deserve to die. We do; for all the wrong that we have done and the hurt that we have caused. But He died in our place so that we could come home to God, freely forgiven. That’s God’s offer of love to each of us. A new life that starts with Jesus.
Reflection Questions

elements.question3: How can you relate to the younger son in your own life journey?
Sin can be fun, yet it is a hollow pleasure that brings destruction. How have you personally experienced this truth about sin?
What have you learned about God from the parable?
What does ’coming home to God’ mean practically for you? What would it look like in an area of life you are struggling with?

chapters.chapterTitle3: Grow in the Father’s Love (part ii)

elements.text3: ‘God loves each of us as if there were only one of us.’ — Augustine of Hippo

elements.question3: What do you think of this quotation? (Do you like it or not, and why?)

elements.text3: The parable of the prodigal son is a story with an aim.

elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>Now all the tax collectors and sinners were coming near to listen to him. <sup>2</sup>But both the Pharisees and the experts in the law were complaining, saying, ‘This man welcomes sinners and eats with them.’

elements.question3: Who was Jesus originally aiming this story at?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The immediate audience includes both groups — the tax collectors and sinners who were drawing near to listen, and the Pharisees and experts in the law who were grumbling about Jesus’ willingness to welcome them. The parable is aimed especially at the religious critics: people who could not understand why a holy man would associate with the wrong sort. The older son in the story is a direct mirror of their attitude.
elements.questionHint3: Luke gives us two groups in these verses — one drawing near to Jesus and one pulling away — which group do you think is most challenged by the parable of the lost son, and why?

elements.text3: Character 3: The Listening Audience
For religious people, this parable’s punch comes with the older son.

elements.passageText5: 28But the older brother became angry and refused to go in. And his father came out and began to urge him.

elements.question3: How does the older son respond when he hears of his brother’s return?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: He becomes angry and refuses to go in to the celebration. Rather than sharing his father’s joy at the return of a lost brother, he stands outside in protest. His response is the mirror image of the father’s: where the father ran towards the returning son, the older brother draws back and separates himself from the family reunion.
elements.questionHint3: While the father ran towards his returning son, the older brother moves in the opposite direction — what does that contrast tell you about the difference between the father’s heart and the older son’s?

elements.passageText5: But the older brother became angry and refused to go in. And his father came out28and began to urge him.

elements.question3: How does his father respond to this response from the older son?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The father comes out — leaving the celebration — and urges the older son to come in. Just as the father ran to the younger son who was far away, he now goes out to the older son who is standing outside in anger. The father’s love is not reserved for only one of his sons; he pursues both. He will not simply accept the older son’s sulk — he actively goes to him and tries to draw him in.
elements.questionHint3: The father left the party to go to the older son — just as he ran to the younger son — what does this pattern of the father always going out tell you about God’s character?

elements.passageText5: <sup>29</sup>But he answered his father, ‘Look! These many years I have served you, and I have never disobeyed your commandment, yet you never gave me a young goat so I could celebrate with my friends. <sup>30</sup>But when this son of yours came back, who has devoured your assets with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him!’

elements.question3: How does the older son respond to his father’s initiative?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: He responds with resentment and a transactional view of his relationship with his father: he has served him for years without disobedience, yet was never rewarded with even a small celebration. He frames everything as a business ledger — his loyal service deserved a return that he never received, while his wasteful brother gets the best. He even refuses to call the younger son his brother, calling him ’this son of yours’. His complaint reveals that he never understood the relationship as one of love at all.
elements.questionHint3: Notice that the older son says he ’served’ his father and ’never disobeyed a commandment’ — does that sound like the language of a son, or of an employee? What does his choice of words reveal about how he understood his relationship with his father?

elements.passageText5: <sup>31</sup>’My child,‘ the father said to him, ‘you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours. <sup>32</sup>But we had to celebrate and rejoice, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again, and was lost and is found!’

elements.question3: What is the key point that his father makes in reply?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The father makes two key points. First, the older son has always had everything — ‘you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours.’ He was never in a transactional relationship; he was already the heir, in constant companionship with his father. He had missed that entirely. Second, the rejoicing was not optional — it was necessary (‘we had to celebrate’), because a dead person had come back to life. The father also pointedly says ’this brother of yours’, gently correcting the older son’s refusal to own the relationship.
elements.questionHint3: The father says ’you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours’ — given that the older son felt deprived and unrewarded, what does this reveal about what he had failed to see or appreciate all along?

elements.text3: The older son doesn’t understand his father. He thinks he has to earn his love. He has done a far better job than his brother and he can’t understand why his father wants anything to do with him. He thinks his relationship with his father is based on reward, not love. He is miles from his father, just as lost as his brother ever was. That’s the shock. If you only knew the start of the story, you would have expected the older son to be in the party and the younger one to be outside. The original audience would have got the message loud and clear: ‘be careful you don’t spend big effort on religion, yet without ever knowing God at all’. Jesus has another listening audience in mind. Us! It forces each of us to ask, ‘Where am I in this story?’

elements.question3: Ask yourself: Am I more like the younger son, or the older one?
Am I ’inside the party’ or ’outside’? Have I come home to God?

elements.text3: People often think Christianity is a reward system. Imagine a multi-story building. At the top you have the ’holy’ people (saints, pastors and so on). After that come all the good people (nurses, doctors and so on). Then you get normal people. And right at the bottom you have the murderers, thieves, rapists, and terrorists. The idea is that, at the end of time, God will draw a horizontal line somewhere; probably a little bit below where we are. If you are above the line, you go to heaven; if you are below the line, you go to hell. That’s a common idea, but it doesn’t work like that at all.

elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>he saved us not by works of righteousness that we have done but on the basis of his mercy, through the washing of the new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit,

elements.question3: How does the Bible contradict that ’tower block’ analogy?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says explicitly that God saved us not because of righteous works we have done, but on the basis of his mercy. The tower block idea assumes that enough good deeds will eventually earn God’s acceptance — but Paul rules that out entirely. Salvation has nothing to do with where we rank on any scale of human goodness; it is entirely a gift of mercy, worked through the new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says God saved us ’not by’ one thing but ’on the basis of’ another — can you identify both, and how does that distinction demolish the idea of a moral ranking system?

elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>yet we know that no one is justified by the works of the law but by the faithfulness of Jesus Christ. And we have come to believe in Christ Jesus, so that we may be justified by the faithfulness of Christ and not by the works of the law, because by the works of the law no one will be justified.

elements.question3: How else does the Bible contradict that ’tower block’ analogy?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul is emphatic and threefold in his denial: no one is justified by works of the law — not partly, not mainly, not at all. Justification comes entirely through the faithfulness of Christ, received by faith in him. The tower block model imagines that law-keeping accumulates merit; Paul says that model produces nothing. Whatever floor of the tower block you occupy, your good deeds cannot justify you before God.
elements.questionHint3: Paul repeats the same negative point three times in this single verse — why do you think he emphasises it so strongly, and what is he trying to rule out completely?

elements.text3: God doesn’t draw a horizontal line. God cuts the tower block vertically. Some at the bottom will spend eternity with Him; some at the top will be shut out. Christianity isn’t about religion, rules, or respectability — it’s about relationship, about knowing God through Jesus. If it were about rules, none of us would get in. God’s standards are far above the top of our building. We can’t enter on our own. God offers to take us back anyway.
God Calls Us Home to Himself
In Luke 15 God paints us a picture of His love. His message to us is one of massive welcome: ‘Wherever you are, whatever you’ve done, come home.’

elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>By this the love of God is revealed in us: that God has sent his one and only Son into the world so that we may live through him. <sup>10</sup>In this is love: not that we have loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the atoning sacrifice for our sins.

elements.question3: How can we know for sure that God loves us?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John points to a concrete, historical event as the proof: God sent his one and only Son into the world so that we might live through him. And crucially, the initiative was entirely God’s — not that we loved God, but that he loved us first and sent his Son as an atoning sacrifice. The love of God is not a feeling or a claim; it is demonstrated in the most costly act imaginable.
elements.questionHint3: John says that love is shown by who acted first — who made the first move in this relationship, God or us, and what did that first move cost?

elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>Who is a God like you, forgiving iniquity and passing over the rebellion of the remnant of his inheritance? He does not remain angry forever, but he delights in loyal love. <sup>19</sup>He will once again have compassion on us; he will subdue our iniquities. You will throw all our sins into the depths of the sea.

elements.question3: How else can we know for sure that God loves us?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Micah describes a God who is unique in his forgiveness — who does not hold onto anger forever but who delights in loyal love. He will have compassion, subdue our sins, and throw them into the depths of the sea, never to be retrieved. This is not reluctant tolerance but active, joyful love — a God who takes pleasure in showing mercy. The completeness of the forgiveness (all our sins, into the sea) is a powerful assurance of his love.
elements.questionHint3: Micah says God ’delights in loyal love’ — the word ’delights’ means he takes pleasure in it. What does that tell you about whether God’s forgiveness is reluctant or wholehearted?

elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>’Come now, let us reason,‘ says the Lord. ’Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow; though they are as red as crimson, they shall be like wool.’

elements.question3: How can we know that God will accept us back, no matter what?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: God himself invites the conversation and makes the offer: sins that are as scarlet — deeply stained, seemingly permanent — will be made as white as snow. The contrast is total: from the deepest red to the purest white. God is not saying that our sins are minor or that they don’t matter; he is saying that no stain is beyond his power to remove. The offer is made to people whose sins are real and serious, and the promise is complete cleansing.
elements.questionHint3: Scarlet and crimson were colours that couldn’t be removed by washing in the ancient world — so why does God choose these specific colours to describe sins he promises to make white as snow?

elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>But if we confess our sins, he is faithful and righteous, forgiving us our sins and cleansing us from all unrighteousness.

elements.question3: How else can we know that God will accept us back, no matter what?
elements.answerPlaceholder1: Amsa tambayar daga cikin nassin littafi mai tsarki
elements.answerPlaceholder2: Jaabo naamnal ngal e nder deftere nde
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John grounds God’s acceptance in his character: he is faithful and righteous, and therefore when we confess, he will forgive and cleanse. This is not a conditional offer that might not hold — it rests on who God is. The forgiveness covers our sins; the cleansing covers ’all unrighteousness’, which is broader still. The one condition on our side is honest confession; everything else is guaranteed by God’s own faithfulness.
elements.questionHint3: John says God forgives because he is ’faithful and righteous’ — rather than saying God forgives because he feels like it. Why does basing forgiveness on God’s character make the promise more reliable?

elements.text3: Fill In the Table Again
Reflecting on what Jesus taught you in His parable, fill in the scale again.

elements.instruction: For each pair of statements, choose the point on the scale that best represents how you now think God sees you, reflecting on what Jesus taught in the parable.

statementPairs.left: He accepts me despite my faults
statementPairs.right: He rejects me because of my faults

statementPairs.left: He has forgiven me for everything
statementPairs.right: He has not forgiven some things
He ignores where my life is going

statementPairs.left: He is passionately interested in me
statementPairs.right: He is not interested in me at all

statementPairs.left: He looks at me with joy
statementPairs.right: He looks at me with disappointment

elements.text3: Reflection Questions

elements.question3: Where are you in this parable?
Have you ever felt like you’ve squandered something valuable in your life? How did you handle it?
How much do you think of God as being like the father? And how much do you think of Him as being like the older brother?
When God looks at you, what does He see? How do you think He feels? What do you imagine He is thinking?

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Grow in Christ
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A seven-session Bible study on growing spiritually — understanding sin, shame, guilt, and the Father’s love.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any chapter.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: Christians who want to grow
blocks.body: If you are already a Christian but feel like your growth has stalled — or if you simply want to grow more — this study is for you. It works through what the Bible says about growing in Jesus: what holds us back, how grace works, and how to get moving again. The goal is not guilt, but growth.

blocks.title: New Christians
blocks.body: If you have recently come to faith, this study will help you get your Christian life off to a strong start. It covers the foundations: repentance and faith, the battle with sin, knowing Christ, and resting in the Father’s love. Working through it carefully will give you something solid to build on.

blocks.title: People exploring Christianity
blocks.body: If you are not yet a Christian but are genuinely curious — wondering what the Christian life actually involves, what it demands, and what it offers — this study is for you too. It doesn’t assume you’ve already made up your mind. It works through what the Bible says and invites you to think carefully about it.

blocks.title: People discipling others
blocks.body: If you are walking alongside someone else in their faith — as a mentor, a small group leader, or a friend — this study gives you a structured way to work through the essentials of spiritual growth together. The seven chapters cover the ground that matters most: what growth looks like, what sin does to us, and what the Father’s love makes possible.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Work through each chapter at your own pace, read the Bible passages, write your answers to the questions, and meditate on the reflection questions at the end. There is no right speed — some chapters may take twenty minutes, others may take longer if a question stops you in your tracks.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study also works well in a small group setting. You could work through each chapter together from scratch, reading the passages and discussing the questions as a group. Alternatively — and this is often the most rewarding approach — each person works through the chapter individually first, writes their answers, and then the group comes together to discuss what they found. Either way works. The questions are designed to prompt genuine reflection, not just right answers.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
It can make a real difference to have someone guiding you through the study — a group leader, a pastor, or a more experienced Christian who can help you think through your answers and questions. Perhaps they are the one who recommended this study to you in the first place.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each chapter has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions are tied to specific passages — asking you to look carefully at what the text says. The reflection questions at the end are more personal — asking what the chapter means for you specifically.

blocks.title: Common Misunderstanding boxes
blocks.body: Throughout the study you will encounter darker-coloured cards headed Common Misunderstanding. These address words — like grace, repentance, or faith — that Christians use all the time but that are often misunderstood. Take a moment to think about what you understand by the word, then tap Find out to see a short clarification.

leadersNotesData.subtitle: Grow in Christ
intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before they help others doing the same.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions in this study are designed to prompt real reflection, not just correct answers. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.

chapters.keyPoints:
This opening chapter establishes the framework for the whole study: the Christian life begins and continues with repentance and faith. Neither can be left behind.

The ‘Growth Graph’ concept is central. Use it to get the group to locate themselves honestly — are they on a Graph 3 trajectory (uneven but growing) or a Graph 4 (stalled)? This is not a trick question; the honest answer is the useful one.

Draw out the link between sin and darkness (1 John 1:5-7): the things we keep hidden tend to grow. Repentance is not just a one-off act at conversion — it is the ongoing posture of the Christian life.
chapters.pastorals:
Some people in the group may feel stuck and ashamed about it. The chapter’s tone is not condemnatory — it is hopeful. Graph 4 can become Graph 5. Make sure that message lands clearly.

Others may be tempted to tick ‘Graph 3’ automatically without real reflection. Encourage honest self-assessment; the growth graph exercise only works if people engage with it genuinely.

Watch for people who are embarrassed about ongoing sin (1 John 1:8-9). The chapter addresses this directly: pretending we have no struggles breeds hypocrisy and stalls growth. Help the group see that acknowledging sin to God — and to safe others — is the path forward, not a sign of failure.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who equate spiritual maturity with having fewer struggles. The chapter argues the opposite: mature Christians are more honest about their struggles, not less.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter explores what it means to grow in Jesus: in obedience, in grace, and in knowledge of Christ. These three are not alternatives — they belong together.

The septic tank illustration is deliberately provocative. Use it to press the question: do we actually want to be saved from sin, or just from its consequences? Salvation and discipleship must go together (Matthew 28:19-20).

The section on grace and godliness (Titus 2:12; Romans 5:6-8, 10) is theologically important. Christ didn’t save us because we learned to obey Him. We obey because He has already saved us. This ordering matters enormously for how people approach the Christian life.
chapters.pastorals:
Paul’s story at the start will resonate with many in the group — the cooling-off pattern is very common. Normalise it, but don’t leave it there: the point is that it doesn’t have to stay that way.

Some may struggle with the idea that God’s love doesn’t depend on their performance. This is deeply counter-intuitive for many people. Take time with Romans 5:6-8 — Christ died for us while we were still sinners, not once we had sorted ourselves out.

The Heart Check Likert scale at the end is designed to surface honest self-awareness. Encourage people to answer it privately and truthfully before any group discussion.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who hear ‘grace’ and conclude that obedience doesn’t matter. The chapter is clear that grace produces godliness (Titus 2:12), not a licence to coast.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter presses the distinction between ageing and maturing. Time passes automatically; growth does not. Some people have been Christians for decades and are still stuck at an early stage of maturity — and that is not what God intends.

The passages from Colossians, Psalm 1, and Mark 4 together build a picture of what roots look like in practice: staying rooted in Christ (Col 2:6-7), meditating on God’s Word (Ps 1:2-3), and not allowing the thorns of life to choke growth (Mark 4:7).

The Gospel-Growth Plan section is the practical centrepiece of the chapter. It asks people to identify one specific, personal growth area — not a vague aspiration but a concrete target. This is worth significant discussion time.
chapters.pastorals:
People may find it uncomfortable to be asked to name a specific growth area. Some will default to something safe and general. Gently push for something real — the exercise only has value if it’s honest.

Be sensitive to the fact that some growth areas (addiction, secret sin, relationship breakdown) may be deeply painful to acknowledge, even privately. The app format gives people privacy; use that as a feature, not a limitation.

The amazing destiny passage (Philippians 3:20-21) is meant to be genuinely motivating. Don’t rush past it — growth is not mere duty; it is moving towards something glorious.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who choose a Gospel-growth area that is really about someone else (‘my marriage’, ‘my family’). The plan should be personal and individual.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter introduces sin as a predator. John Owen’s quote — ‘Be killing sin or it will be killing you’ — frames everything that follows. Sin is not a minor inconvenience; it is actively destructive.

The study of Genesis 3 repays careful attention. The serpent’s tactics — questioning God’s word, distorting His commands, promising what He hasn’t promised — are still in use today. Help the group see how these tactics work in their own experience of temptation.

The section on sin spreading (Genesis 4) shows how quickly sin escalates: from one act of disobedience to fratricide within a generation. The weed-seed illustration makes the point well — small sins are not harmless; they grow.
chapters.pastorals:
Thomas’s story at the start is deliberately confronting. Some in the group may have their own version of it. The aim is not to shock, but to be honest that sin’s effects can be devastating — and that there is a way back.

The question ‘In your life, who is doing better at killing who?’ is intentionally direct. Give people space to sit with that question before moving on. Silence here is often productive.

Watch for people who minimise their sin by comparing it to others’. The half-truth dynamic (Genesis 3) is worth pressing: the most dangerous lies have some truth mixed in.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who have carried hidden sin for a long time and may feel that their situation is beyond hope. The hints of grace in Genesis 3:15 and 3:21 are there for a reason — God has not given up on His purposes of blessing.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter explores sin as refusal — refusal of God’s knowledge, His authority, His goodness, and ultimately of God Himself. Reinhold Niebuhr’s opening quote frames sin as the refusal to accept our creaturely limits.

The sequence of passages builds a cumulative picture of what sin refuses: knowledge (Romans 1:18-23), God’s authority (Romans 5:14; Ezekiel 2:3-5), gratitude (Romans 1:21b), relationship (Leviticus 26:40), and finally God Himself (Romans 1:25).

The contrast with Mark 12:30 is important: the opposite of refusing God is loving Him with everything. Use this to move the discussion from diagnosis to aspiration.

The closing bridge text on sin hierarchies is worth dwelling on — Christians often tolerate invisible sins (pride, greed, envy) while policing visible ones. Jesus didn’t operate that way.
chapters.pastorals:
This chapter is theologically dense. Don’t feel you need to cover every passage in a group setting — pick the two or three that are most relevant to where your group is.

The question ‘Which of these types of refusals are you most at risk of?’ is very personal. It may produce either very honest answers or very guarded ones. Either response is revealing.

The Pharisee dynamic in the closing bridge (Mark 2:15-17) is important pastorally: some group members may be very good at external religion while harbouring deep interior refusals. Handle with care.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who identify easily with the ‘refusal of authority’ examples but are blind to their own refusal of gratitude or relationship with God. Both are forms of the same thing.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter turns from the diagnosis of sin to the Father’s love. The parable of the lost son (Luke 15:11-32) is the primary text, explored through the lens of its three main characters.

The younger son represents the person who has walked away from God openly. His journey — from demanding his inheritance to feeding pigs to ‘coming to his senses’ — maps onto many people’s experience of sin and its consequences.

The father’s response is the theological heart of the chapter: he sees his son ‘while he was still a long way off’, runs to him, and restores him fully. This is not a grudging acceptance — it is extravagant welcome. The robe, the ring, the fattened calf: full restoration, not probation.

The bipolar Likert scale at the start — measuring how the group perceives God’s attitude towards them — sets up a before/after comparison with chapter 7. It’s worth completing carefully.
chapters.pastorals:
Some in the group will identify painfully with the younger son. Be ready for that. The chapter’s message is one of hope: the father hasn’t given up, and neither has God.

The distinction between leaving through the ‘front door’ and leaving through the ‘back door’ is pastorally significant. Some people in church settings have never actually come home to God — they’ve just stayed in the vicinity. Handle this gently but honestly.

The question ‘Do you mostly feel at home with God, or more distant?’ is a key pastoral barometer. Don’t rush past it.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who have a deeply negative view of God — as distant, judgmental, or indifferent. The parable is specifically designed to correct that picture. Let it do its work.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter completes the parable by focusing on the older son and the listening audience. Jesus originally told the parable to the Pharisees — people who were religious but distant from God in a different way.

The older son’s problem is not irreligion but a transactional view of his relationship with the father. He thinks he has earned love through obedience. He is miles from his father emotionally even while living in his house. This is a searching challenge for churchgoing people.

The ‘tower block’ illustration challenges the common assumption that salvation is about being good enough. Titus 3:5 and Galatians 2:16 make clear that no one is justified by works — God cuts the building vertically, not horizontally.

The repeated bipolar Likert scale at the end allows people to compare their perception of God’s attitude towards them before and after engaging with the parable. This can be a powerful moment of reflection.
chapters.pastorals:
The older son dynamic is often harder to surface in a group than the younger son dynamic. People who identify with the older son are often high-functioning, respectable, and resistant to the idea that they might be ‘outside the party’.

The questions ‘Am I more like the younger son or the older one?’ and ‘Am I inside the party or outside?’ are the most important in the chapter. Give them real time and real space.

The closing comparison of the two Likert scales (from chapters 6 and 7) is an opportunity for a genuinely moving moment — if people have engaged honestly, they may find that the parable has shifted something in how they see God. Be attentive to that.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who give intellectual assent to the gospel (‘yes, I know God loves me’) without any emotional reality to it. The parable is designed to move people, not just inform them.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
